author: @Alysinan
text: I’m super late to this video but I am a bit confused and wondering if anyone had any answers. What is the point of using a coil to shut off a magnet? Wouldn’t it be more efficient to use that same amount of electrical power in directly making an electromagnet? I guess my question is how much Gauss would you need to induce in a coil to completely turn off a permanent magnet? If it takes 100 gauss in the coil to turn off a 100 gauss permanent magnet then what is the point when you can use that same coil directly as a magnet? Sorry for my confusion I’m not even sure if my question makes sense

author: @nickbaylander6320
text: Theirs another way to do this. If you put 2 magnets on a small piece of steel facing the same direction the steel will be magnetized. But if you flip one of the magnets over. The steel won't be magnetized any more. Also could you please do a demonstration on the halbach array

author: @nickbaylander6320
text: The permanent magnets wont last long when they forced into opposite polls that are stronger than the the permanent magnets. I've messed up a few magnets that way but they can easily be fixed with the right type of magneto. Permanent magnets can be recharged

author: @leskraut2462
text: you really have my attention Robert. Questions.. Since the iron rods are saturated then it appears all your doing is passing the coil magnetism(which even at modest voltages are far more powerful than magnets) to the ends of the shafts. That wouldn't be flux switching, just flux passing? however if you are suggesting that the coil is somehow gating the flux of the magnets to the end of the rods then that would really be something. So what is actually happening in the rods? is it like a pond that is full, and when you put more water in, it simply overflows the same volume at the other side? or are you putting pressure on a valve at the other side allowing the pond to suddenly and quickly empty? This is very exciting and reminds me of the radius boots. However your explanation and simple to copy experiment seems very intriguing! on my way to the hardware store...

author: @ScarletFlames1
text:
What I'm getting from this is that it's a magnetic flux diverter and by magnetic assist you actually mean that. This would be really useful in motors and generators to increase efficiency and maximum power output at the cost of size and weight. could be used to change the form factor of a motor as well as it allows you to move the magnets out of the internals of the motor (or the casing) thus allowing you to make longer but much thinner motors that have the same strength.

Though I don't see this becoming useful in things like cars or wind turbines, steam generators could definitely stand to gain from this as it allows to more easily thermally insulate the magnets from superheated steam without using gigantic and expensive mechanical systems and bearings to separate it.

author: @JamesThompson-xl4yu
text: I also was thinking along this design for a permanent magnet motor, where the coil simply pulls back the stater field in short duration to allow the armature time to transit the field of stater to armature magnets. the coils at the stater's are not to turn the motor, but it is the magnets attraction and repulsion the turns it.  What do you think?

author: @olivierroy1301
text: Hi, I was reading about farads and tried to compare it to amps and was not able to sort things out. Some peoples were discussing about the definition where 1 amp=coulombs and farads is colomb-second where coulomb multiplies with seconds, unlike km/h.  Is there a  simple definition you could explain please?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it will be a bit long winded so I would happily do a video but an amp is a coulomb per second - a farad is one coulomb at one volt - that's because an amp measure a rate of flow so to speak and a farad measures an amount of charge - it's a bit like how much water is there in this bucket then how much is flowing past my feet if I knock a hole in it

replies.author: @olivierroy1301
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So what I understand is that they both represent a capacity, but they are not expressed the same way.. amp is a capacity of charge for a period and farad is the charge. If I'm wrong, at least I have a visual representation. Thanks.

author: @krkfire-976
text: The flux field will remain until you break the bond between the his show and iron bar.  It is in essence a flux capacitor.

replies.author: @krkfire-976
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It is edward leedskanin's design from his book Magnetic Currents.  It would be fun if you did a video.  A lot of people have done YouTube videos on this subject  but it would neet to see your take on it.

author: @krkfire-976
text: Next, you need to make a flux capacitor.  1 iron horseshoe - 1 iron bar - 2 wire coils.  You can engage the flux field with a 9v battery to hold the iron bar in place.  remove the battery

author: @kenrowe167
text: Hi Rob. A good while back, you posted a video on what you called (I think) a flux gate switch, where you wound a coil around a (soft magnetic) ceramic magnet and magnetically coupled it to a (hard magnetic) neodymium magnet. In that video, you achieved a similar  effect to what you're demonstrating now by simply pulsing the current through the coil and reversing the magnetisation of the ceramic magnet. I looked, but I can't find that video now. You said at the time that you were thinking of using the effect to build a motor. Did you ever try it?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That was a while ago mate - the effect is pretty much the same but the switch speed is slow and the realignment of the soft magnetic material - alnico - had too high an energy cost - but on the plus side the effect was long lasting
